messages.content: narrate the hickorey dickory dock poem

Here's a narration of the Hickory Dickory Dock poem:

The poem starts with "Hickory, dickory, dock" which is repeated throughout. A mouse runs up the clock, and when it strikes one, the mouse runs down again.

Then, the same sequence happens again, but this time the clock strikes two, and instead of running down, the mouse "flew" down (or possibly fell) Hickory, dickory dock.
in which poem the mouse and the clock is involved?
The mouse and the clock are involved in the poem "Hickory Dickory Dock".
what is the second poem?
The second poem is "London Bridge".
tell  me the full poem of LITTLE BUNNY FOO FOO

Here is the full poem of LITTLE BUNNY FOO FOO:

Little Bunny Foo Foo,
Hoping through the forest,
Scooping up the field mice,
And Bopping' 'em on the head.

(Spoken) And down came the good fairy and she said:
"Little Bunny Foo Foo, I don't want to see you
Scooping up the field mice
And Bopping' 'em on the head.
I'll give you three chances,
And if you don't behave I'll turn you into a GOON!"

The next day same as before except:
Two chances...
One chance...
Last verse say: 'Little Bunny Foo Foo, I gave you three chances, POOF!
Now your a GOON!'
which poem comes after the poem this little piggy??
The poem "Lavender's Blue" comes after the poem "This Little Piggy".
in that old man poem what does he played with?
The Old Man played "knick-knack" on various parts of his body and others' bodies, but initially it says he played one knick-knock on the narrator's thumb.
what is knick knack?

According to the traditional nursery rhyme "THIS OLD MAN", a "knick-knack" refers to something that is played or made on a specific body part, such as the thumb (on my thumb), shoe (on my shoe), knee (on my knee), door (on my door), hive (on my hive), sticks (on my sticks), heaven (up to heaven), gate (on my gate), all the time (nine), or once again (ten). In each case, it's accompanied by a "patty whack" and a gift of a bone for a dog.
list all the poem names as per the order

Here are the poem names in the order they appear:

1. This Little Piggy
2. LAVENDAR'S BLUE (Lavender's Blue)
3. FUZZY WUZZY
4. TWO LITTLE FEET
5. Hickety Pickety Bumblebee
6. HERE IS A BUNNY
7. Sing a Song of Sixpence
8. TISKET, A TASKET
9. A Hunting We Will Go
10. London Bridge
list all the poem names in the order..also give summary for each rhymes

This Little Piggy, went to market This Little Piggy, stayed home This Little Piggy, had roast beef This Little Piggy, had none But this little Piggy, cried wee wee wee All the way home LAVENDAR’S BLUE Lavender's blue, dilly dilly, lavender's green, When I am king, dilly, dilly, you shall be queen
what is the story behind the rhyme the lavenders blue?

The rhyme "Lavender's Blue" appears to be a traditional English folk song or nursery rhyme. The lyrics suggest that it's about two people who are in love and are awaiting their future together.

In the rhyme, the speaker says "When I am king, dilly, dilly, you shall be queen." This suggests that the speaker is anticipating a future where they will hold power and authority, and they believe that their loved one will share this status with them. The lines "Who told you so, dilly, dilly, who told you so? 'Twas my own heart, dilly, dilly, that told me so" imply that the speaker's love is a source of conviction or certainty for their future together.

The rhyme also touches on themes of work and domesticity, with the lines "Call up your men, dilly, dilly, set them to work / Some with a rake, dilly, dilly, some with a fork" suggesting a vision of a harmonious and productive life in which both partners contribute to their shared future.

However, it's worth noting that the origins and exact meaning of this rhyme are not explicitly stated in the provided document content. The song has been interpreted in various ways over time, and its true significance may be lost to history.
